navigation_overview: Parabank is a sample banking application. When visitors arrive, they see the sign-in page, which offers an Email / Username and Password form, a Register link for new customers, and a Forgot Password? link for credential recovery. After a successful log-in, users reach their account dashboard displaying every account and balance. The left-hand navigation menu now contains the following options: ① Accounts Overview (default dashboard view), ② Open New Account, ③ Transfer Funds, ④ Bill Pay, ⑤ Request Loan, ⑥ Update Contact Info, ⑦ Manage Cards, ⑧ Investments, ⑨ Account Statements, ⑩ Security Settings, ⑪ Support Center, and ⑫ Log Out.

module_summaries.summary:
Line 1: Users can sign in with email/username and password or access the Forgot Password flow.
Line 2: Displays the login form, validation errors, and authentication result messages (e.g., "Signed in successfully.").

Line 1: Users can create a new customer account by submitting the full registration form.
Line 2: Displays registration fields, inline validation errors, and registration success/confirmation messages.

Line 1: Users can view an accounts dashboard listing all customer accounts and totals.
Line 2: Displays masked account numbers, account type, current balance, account status badges, open date, and total balance footer.

Line 1: Users can open a new Checking or Savings account by selecting type, entering an initial deposit, and choosing a funding source.
Line 2: Displays account type cards with features/minimums, deposit and funding-account fields, real-time validation errors, and success confirmation.

Line 1: Users can transfer funds internally between their accounts or initiate external transfers by entering destination account details.
Line 2: Displays transfer type options, source/destination fields, transfer amount, validation errors, and transfer result with balance updates.

Line 1: Users can submit bill payments by entering payee details, account numbers, payment amount, and selecting a source account.
Line 2: Displays payee and payment fields, validation errors, payment confirmation with reference code, and updated account balances.

Line 1: Users can apply for Personal, Auto, or Home loans by selecting loan type, entering loan and down payment amounts and choosing collateral.
Line 2: Displays loan type cards with interest rates, allowed amount ranges, collateral selection, validation feedback, and approval/denial result.

Line 1: Users can edit and update their contact information via a pre-filled profile form and submit changes.
Line 2: Displays pre-filled contact fields (name, address, phone), inline validation errors, and profile update confirmation.

Line 1: Users can request new debit/credit cards and manage existing card controls (limits, travel notices, freeze/unfreeze).
Line 2: Displays card request and control forms, existing card list and statuses, request tracking IDs, and confirmation messages.

Line 1: Users can view a portfolio snapshot and execute buy or sell trades for funds using the trade form.
Line 2: Displays read-only holdings, market value and unrealised gain/loss, plus trade inputs and trade execution results/validation.

Line 1: Users can generate account statements for a selected period and set e-statement (paperless) preferences.
Line 2: Displays statement period/account inputs, retrieved transaction lists on success, and e-statement preference/email settings.

Line 1: Users can change their account password by entering current and new password values and submitting the form.
Line 2: Displays the change-password fields, validation errors for strength/mismatch, and success/failure messages.

Line 1: Users can send secure support messages (with optional attachment) and request scheduled callbacks with preferred date/time.
Line 2: Displays message and callback forms, category/reason selectors, inline validation, and ticket/callback confirmation IDs.

test_cases.title: Sign in with valid email and password
test_cases.workflow: Submit the form and validate the provided credentials against the registered-user database
test_cases.preconditions: A registered user exists with a valid email and password
steps: Fill all required fields (Email/Username with a valid email address, Password with a valid password meeting complexity rules)
Click "Sign In"
test_cases.expected_result: User is redirected to the Accounts Overview page and the system flashes "Signed in successfully."

test_cases.workflow: Submit the form and validate the provided credentials against the registered-user database

test_cases.expected_result: User is redirected to the Accounts Overview page and the system flashes "Signed in successfully."

test_cases.title: Register successfully with valid required data

test_cases.title: Register successfully using ZIP Code in 5+4 format

steps: Fill all required fields with valid values using a ZIP Code in 5+4 format (ZIP+4)
Click "Register"
test_cases.expected_result: Account created successfully and user is redirected to the login page.

test_cases.title: Welcome message shows the user's name

steps: Locate the welcome message area on the Accounts Overview page
Verify the welcome message includes the logged-in user's name

test_cases.title: Accounts table displays expected columns in each row

steps: Locate the accounts table on the page
Verify each table row shows Account Number (clickable but not implemented yet), Account Type, Current Balance, Account Status (Active badge), and Open Date

test_cases.title: Footer displays the total balance across all accounts

test_cases.title: Rows ordered by account creation date (earliest first)

steps: Inspect the Open Date values in the table rows from top to bottom
Verify the sequence of Open Date values is in ascending order (earliest first)
test_cases.expected_result: Table rows are ordered by account creation date with the earliest open date first.

test_cases.title: Account numbers are masked showing only last four digits
test_cases.preconditions: User is logged in and Accounts Overview page is visible.
steps: Locate the Account Number values in the table
Verify each Account Number is masked for security, displaying only the last four digits
test_cases.expected_result: All Account Number values are masked except for the last four digits (e.g., ****1234).

test_cases.title: Open a new Checking account with valid initial deposit
test_cases.preconditions: User is logged in and Open New Account page is open.
steps: Select Account type = Checking and fill all required fields (Initial Deposit Amount: valid amount above checking minimum, Funding Source Account: account with sufficient balance)
Click "Open Account"
test_cases.expected_result: "Account opened successfully!" is displayed and user is redirected to accounts overview; the new checking account appears in the accounts listing.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the newly opened Checking account appears in the Accounts Overview listing with type Checking, a masked account number, and the initial balance equal to the requested deposit.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Accounts table displays expected columns in each row
post_verifications.execution_note: Run this Accounts Overview test after opening the account: navigate/refresh to Accounts Overview, locate the accounts table, filter or sort by Open Date or Account Type=Checking (or inspect recent rows), find the new row and verify the Account Type is 'Checking', the Account Number is masked (only last 4 digits visible), and the Current Balance equals the initial deposit amount supplied when opening the account.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify funds were transferred from the selected funding source by confirming the funding account's available balance decreased by the initial deposit amount.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Footer displays the total balance across all accounts
post_verifications.execution_note: Use the Accounts Overview page to record the funding account's Current Balance before opening the new account, then run again after the open-account action and compare. Identify the funding account row (by account number or masked number) and record its Current Balance value. The before_after harness will compute the difference and verify it equals the initial deposit amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record funding source account available balance prior to opening new account.
post_verifications.after_action: Record funding source account available balance after account opening and verify it decreased by the deposit amount.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the new checking account's balance reflects the deposited amount (complements the funding account debit).

post_verifications.execution_note: On the Accounts Overview page, locate the row for the newly opened Checking account (e.g., by Account Type = Checking and the most recent Open Date or the account number returned by the create action). Read the 'Current Balance' value displayed in that row and assert it equals the known initial deposit amount used when opening the account.

test_cases.title: Open a new Savings account with valid initial deposit

Because the execution_strategy is after_only, the test must by itself confirm the new record and expected balance — this test does not currently perform that confirmation.

Locate the new row and verify: 1) Account Type column shows 'Savings'; 2) Account Number is masked (only last four digits visible); 3) Current Balance equals the initial deposit amount entered when opening the account.

Compare the two values to confirm the balance decreased by the initial deposit amount. Specifically: locate the funding source account row (by account number or masked number), read the Current Balance field and record it (before); perform the Open New Account action (source test 4.ONA-002); re-open Accounts Overview and read the same account's Current Balance (after).
post_verifications.before_action: Record funding source account available balance prior to opening new account.
post_verifications.after_action: Record funding source account available balance after account opening and verify it decreased by the deposit amount.

test_cases.title: Successful internal transfer between own accounts
test_cases.preconditions: User is logged in, Transfer Funds page is open, and user has at least two own accounts (Checking or Savings) with sufficient balance
steps: Select the radio button for internal transfer (My ParaBank Account)

Fill all required fields (select Source Account from the Source Account dropdown, select Destination internal account from the destination options, enter a valid Transfer Amount within available balance)
Click the "Transfer" or "Submit" button
test_cases.expected_result: Confirmation message "Transfer completed successfully." is displayed and a transaction ID is shown for the transfer.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the source account's balance decreased by the transfer amount.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Footer displays the total balance across all accounts
post_verifications.execution_note: Run this Accounts Overview test before the transfer to record the 'Current Balance' value for the source account row (identify by account number or name). After performing the internal transfer, run it again to read the source account's Current Balance from the same row. Compute (before_balance - after_balance) and verify it equals the transfer amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record source account available balance prior to transfer.
post_verifications.after_action: Record source account available balance after transfer and verify decrease equals transfer amount.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the destination internal account's balance increased by the transfer amount.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Footer displays the total balance across all accounts
post_verifications.execution_note: Use this Accounts Overview test to read and record the destination account's Current Balance (available balance) from the account row. Run the test once BEFORE the transfer to capture the baseline balance and again AFTER the transfer to capture the updated balance. Compare the two recorded balances; the difference should equal the transfer amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record destination account available balance prior to transfer.
post_verifications.after_action: Record destination account available balance after transfer and verify increase equals transfer amount.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify a transfer record exists with the shown transaction ID in the Transfer Funds records/history.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Destination options update when changing transfer type
post_verifications.execution_note: This test runs on the Transfer Funds page and checks destination options when switching transfer types, but it does not open transfer history or a transfer details page to look up a transaction ID. It therefore cannot, by itself, confirm that a transfer record with the submitted transaction ID exists.
post_verifications.reason: All three candidate tests operate on the Transfer Funds page and their module action_states include 'funds_transfer' (they exercise the transfer action rather than observe history). Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate whose action_states overlap the source test's modifies_state should not be marked 'found' because it performs the same action instead of verifying the result. None of the candidates open transfer history or a transfer-details view or search by transaction ID (they do not access/display the required transfer record or its transaction ID), so none fully satisfies the after_only verification requirement.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: After performing the transfer (when you have the transaction ID): 1) Navigate to Transfer Funds -> Transfer History or Transactions/Account Activity for the source account. 2) Use the search/filter or open transfer history and locate the record with the transaction ID shown at submission. 3) Open the transfer details and confirm the transaction ID, amount, source account, destination account, and transfer_status match the submitted values.

coverage_gaps: All three candidate tests operate on the Transfer Funds page and their module action_states include 'funds_transfer' (they exercise the transfer action rather than observe history). Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate whose action_states overlap the source test's modifies_state should not be marked 'found' because it performs the same action instead of verifying the result. None of the candidates open transfer history or a transfer-details view or search by transaction ID (they do not access/display the required transfer record or its transaction ID), so none fully satisfies the after_only verification requirement.

After performing the transfer, run the same test again and record the same field from the same account row. The before_after harness will compare the two recorded balances to verify the decrease equals the transfer amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record source account available balance prior to external transfer.
post_verifications.after_action: Record source account available balance after transfer and verify decrease equals transfer amount.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify an external transfer request/record was created for the provided destination account number (external transfer request persists in Transfer Funds records).
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Destination options update when changing transfer type
post_verifications.execution_note: This test operates in the Transfer Funds module and exercises the external-account input UI (it can observe the external_account_entry affordance), but it does not open the transfer/request history or external-transfer queue to confirm that a persisted external transfer request was created. Because its action_states overlap with the source test's modifies_state (external_transfer_request), it cannot be marked as a full after-only verifier. To make it a full verification, extend the test after submission to navigate to Transfer Funds -> Transfer/Request History (or External Transfer Queue) and search for a record matching the submitted destination account number and amount.
post_verifications.reason: The candidate runs in the correct module and touches external transfer inputs, but it only validates UI input/option behavior rather than confirming persistence of a new external transfer record. Also its module action_states overlap the source modification (external_transfer_request), so it appears to be an action/form test rather than an observation of the resulting stored record.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: After performing the external transfer (source test), open Transfer Funds → Transfer/Request History or External Transfer Queue. Filter or search by the destination account number and transfer amount. Verify an external transfer request record exists that references the provided destination account number, the submitted amount, and the source account. Record the transfer/request ID and status (external_account_entry) as evidence.

coverage_gaps: The candidate runs in the correct module and touches external transfer inputs, but it only validates UI input/option behavior rather than confirming persistence of a new external transfer record. Also its module action_states overlap the source modification (external_transfer_request), so it appears to be an action/form test rather than an observation of the resulting stored record.

test_cases.title: Submit valid payment and verify reference code and balance update
test_cases.preconditions: User is logged in and the Submit Payment form is open
steps: Fill all required fields (Payee Name, Street Address, City, State, ZIP Code, Phone Number, Payee Account Number, Confirm Account Number) with matching valid values and a valid payee contact
Fill Payment Amount with an amount less than the selected source account's available balance and select a Source Account from the dropdown
Click "Pay"
test_cases.expected_result: Payment submitted successfully and a reference code is displayed; the source account balance is reduced accordingly and updated on the page.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the source account's balance decreased by the payment amount after submitting the bill payment.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Footer displays the total balance across all accounts
post_verifications.execution_note: Run this Accounts Overview test before and after the payment. Locate the selected source account's row and read the Current/Available Balance value (record it before payment). After submitting the payment, refresh or reopen Accounts Overview and read the same account's Current/Available Balance again. Compare the two values — the decrease should equal the payment amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record source account available balance prior to payment submission.
post_verifications.after_action: Record source account available balance after payment and verify decrease equals payment amount.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify a payment record with the referenced payment code exists and contains matching payee, amount, and source account details.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Submit payment when amount equals available funds (boundary)
post_verifications.execution_note: This Payments test does exercise payment submission and can observe payment_reference and payee details, but it itself performs a create action (module action_states include bill_payment, account_balance). Because the required verification is after_only (must confirm the record exists after the original action), you should NOT reuse this test verbatim as the verifier. Instead use it only as a reference for the fields to check, or adapt it into a read-only verification that opens Payment History/Records and looks up the displayed reference code.
post_verifications.reason: The candidate is in the correct module and exposes the relevant states (payment_reference, payee_details, account_balance), but it performs the same create action as the source test (overlapping action_states: bill_payment, account_balance). Per the hard module rule, a test that performs the action cannot be marked 'found' as the verification — we need a read-only/observational test that can confirm the payment record exists after the submit action.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: After the payment submission that produced the reference code, navigate to Payments -> Payment History (or Records). Search or filter by the displayed reference code. Open the matching payment record and assert: (1) the reference code matches the displayed code, (2) payee name and payee account details match the submitted payee, (3) the payment amount equals the submitted amount, and (4) the Source Account shown matches the selected source account. If automated, implement a read-only test that performs these lookup and assertions (do not re-submit a payment).

coverage_gaps: The candidate is in the correct module and exposes the relevant states (payment_reference, payee_details, account_balance), but it performs the same create action as the source test (overlapping action_states: bill_payment, account_balance). Per the hard module rule, a test that performs the action cannot be marked 'found' as the verification — we need a read-only/observational test that can confirm the payment record exists after the submit action.

test_cases.title: Request a Personal loan with valid inputs
test_cases.preconditions: User is signed in and Request Loan page is open.
steps: Select Loan Type = Personal and fill all required fields (Loan Amount within personal range, Down Payment at or above minimum percentage but less than Loan Amount, select Collateral Account with sufficient funds, choose Interest rate)
Click "Submit Loan Request"
test_cases.expected_result: "Loan approved and created successfully!" message is displayed and the new loan account details are shown.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify a new Personal loan application/loan record exists and is recorded as approved/created in the Request Loan records with the chosen terms.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Accept Personal loan request when Loan Amount equals the Personal minimum
post_verifications.execution_note: This candidate operates in the Request Loan module and checks loan_application_status and loan_terms, so it is relevant. However, its defined steps perform a loan submission (action_states overlap with the source modifies_state), so as written it is not a pure observer and cannot serve as an after_only verification test by itself. To use it for verification, replace the submission steps with navigation to the Request Loan history or the newly created loan detail page and assert the presence and fields of the recently created Personal loan record.
post_verifications.reason: The candidate test submits a Personal loan request (its action_states include loan_creation, collateral_hold, loan_status), so it performs the same state-changing action as the source test rather than observing the result. The execution_strategy is after_only and requires a test that can confirm the new record exists and shows type='Personal', approved/created status, and the chosen terms. The candidate can verify the required states but must be adapted to only read/display the loan record rather than creating one.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: After running the Request-a-Personal-loan action (7.REQLOA-001), log into Request Loan -> Loan History (or open the new loan's detail page). Locate the most recent record and verify: (1) Loan Type is 'Personal'; (2) loan_application_status shows 'Approved' or 'Created' as expected; (3) the loan terms match the selected values (Loan Amount, Interest Rate, Down Payment). If possible, filter by applicant or submission time to find the exact record.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the selected collateral account reflects the collateral hold/reservation (related account shows hold or reserved amount) after loan creation.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Accounts table displays expected columns in each row
post_verifications.execution_note: Run this Accounts Overview row/column check before and after loan submission to record the collateral account's Current Balance and Account Status from the account row. This captures the per-account balance value that is needed for the before/after comparison, but it does not explicitly capture a hold/reservation indicator or reserved amount.
post_verifications.before_action: Record collateral account balance and any hold/reservation indicator prior to loan request.
post_verifications.after_action: Record collateral account balance and hold/reservation indicator after loan creation and verify the hold/reservation is present or reserved amount recorded.
post_verifications.reason: The candidate is in the required Accounts Overview module and reads per-account Current Balance and status, but its listed verifiable states do not include a hold/reservation indicator or reserved_amount. Because the verification requires observing a hold/reserved indicator or reserved amount for the collateral account, this test alone is insufficient.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: Before submitting the loan, on Accounts Overview locate the specific collateral account row and record Current Balance and any visible hold/reservation fields. If the overview does not show hold/reservation, click into the account detail or Holds/Reservations view and record the 'held' or 'reserved' amount and available balance. Repeat the same steps after loan creation and compare the recorded values to confirm the hold/reservation was placed.

coverage_gaps: The candidate test submits a Personal loan request (its action_states include loan_creation, collateral_hold, loan_status), so it performs the same state-changing action as the source test rather than observing the result. The execution_strategy is after_only and requires a test that can confirm the new record exists and shows type='Personal', approved/created status, and the chosen terms. The candidate can verify the required states but must be adapted to only read/display the loan record rather than creating one.

The candidate is in the required Accounts Overview module and reads per-account Current Balance and status, but its listed verifiable states do not include a hold/reservation indicator or reserved_amount. Because the verification requires observing a hold/reserved indicator or reserved amount for the collateral account, this test alone is insufficient.

test_cases.title: Request an Auto loan with valid inputs
test_cases.preconditions: User is signed in and Request Loan page is open.
steps: Select Loan Type = Auto and fill all required fields (Loan Amount within auto range, Down Payment at or above minimum percentage but less than Loan Amount, select Collateral Account with sufficient funds, choose Interest rate)
Click "Submit Loan Request"
test_cases.expected_result: "Loan approved and created successfully!" message is displayed and the new loan account details are shown.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify a new Auto loan application/loan record exists and is recorded as approved/created in the Request Loan records with the chosen terms.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Reject Auto loan request when Loan Amount is above allowed maximum
post_verifications.execution_note: This test runs in the Request Loan module and its can_verify_states includes loan_application_status and loan_terms (which are the right data to read). However 7.REQLOA-009 performs a submission action (and expects a rejection validation) rather than viewing Request Loan history or the loan detail page. For after_only verification you need a test that, after the create action completes, opens the Request Loan history or the created loan detail and asserts a record exists with type='Auto', status='approved/created', and the chosen terms. To reuse 7.REQLOA-009 you would need to replace its submission/validation steps with steps that navigate to the Request Loan history or loan detail and assert the Auto loan record and fields are present.
post_verifications.reason: Although this candidate touches the correct module and exposes the relevant state fields (loan_application_status, loan_terms), it is itself an action-oriented test that submits a loan request (and in this case expects rejection). Per the rules, tests whose action_states overlap the source's modifies_state cannot be marked 'found' because they perform the same action rather than observing the result. Also the expected outcome of 7.REQLOA-009 (validation error) is the opposite of the desired verification (an approved/created Auto loan record).
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: After running the Request an Auto loan action (7.REQLOA-002), as a verifier open Request Loan -> History (or the newly created loan's detail page). Click "Save Preference"
test_cases.expected_result: Displays 'e-Statement preference updated.' and the paperless preference is saved (checkbox remains selected and the entered email is shown).

post_verifications.ideal: Record the current paperless checkbox state and email; after saving the e-Statement preference, refresh the preferences page and re-open the account (and perform a re-login) to confirm the paperless checkbox remains selected and the entered email persists.
post_verifications.before_action: Record existing paperless checkbox state and stored email (if any) from Account Statements preferences.
post_verifications.after_action: After saving, refresh the page and then log out and log back in; re-open Account Statements preferences and verify checkbox is selected and the entered email is present.
post_verifications.reason: All candidate tests are in other modules (Login, Open New Account) and their can_verify_states do not overlap with the modified state e_statement_preference. Per the Hard Module Rule, a candidate from a different module can only be used if its can_verify_states overlaps with e_statement_preference — none do. Therefore none can serve as the before/after observer for Account Statements preferences.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step:
Create or run a test that opens the Account Statements > Preferences page and records the following (this is sufficient for before_after execution):
1) Log in as the test user.
2) Navigate to Account Statements -> Preferences (or Account Statements preferences page).
3) Record the current state of the 'Paperless statements' checkbox (checked/unchecked) and the value in the 'Email Address' field.
(Record these values as the BEFORE snapshot.)
4) Perform the source action: check the paperless checkbox, enter the valid email, and click 'Save Preference'.
5) After saving, refresh the Preferences page and observe the checkbox and email field — record these values.
6) Log out, then log back in as the same user, re-open Account Statements -> Preferences and record the checkbox state and email field again (AFTER snapshot).
7) Compare BEFORE and AFTER snapshots: the paperless checkbox should be selected and the entered email should persist across refresh and re-login.
Note: Because the strategy is before_after, the test only needs to display/read these values (no automated assertion of change is required by the verifier).

coverage_gaps: All candidate tests are in other modules (Login, Open New Account) and their can_verify_states do not overlap with the modified state e_statement_preference. Per the Hard Module Rule, a candidate from a different module can only be used if its can_verify_states overlaps with e_statement_preference — none do. Therefore none can serve as the before/after observer for Account Statements preferences.

test_cases.title: Change password with valid current and strong matching new password
test_cases.preconditions: User is authenticated and Security Settings > Change Password panel is open
steps: Expand the Change Password panel if collapsed
Fill all required fields (Current Password: valid current password, New Password: valid strong password, Confirm New Password: same as New Password)
Click "Change Password"
test_cases.expected_result: Password changed successfully and credentials are updated.

post_verifications.ideal: Attempt authentication using the old/current password and then using the new password after the change: the old password must fail to authenticate and the new password must succeed.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: After changing password the old password no longer works
post_verifications.execution_note: Run this test AFTER the change-password action (the source test 12.SECSET-001). Do NOT re-run the password-change step included in the test case; instead, start at the 'Log out' / 'Attempt to log in' steps. Attempt to sign in with the previous (old) password (expect authentication denied), then attempt to sign in with the new password (expect authentication success and an active session).

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the password change persists across logout/login by logging out and then logging back in with the new password and confirming access to the Accounts Overview dashboard (account list and balances visible).
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Welcome message shows the user's name
post_verifications.execution_note: This Accounts Overview test verifies post-login UI (welcome message and account-related data) and thus can demonstrate that a user session is established and account_list is accessible — but it does NOT perform the logout/login step or attempt authentication with the new password. To use it for the required verification, first perform the logout and sign-in with the new password, then run this test to confirm the Accounts Overview (welcome message, account list and balances) is visible.
post_verifications.reason: The requirement (after_only) needs a test that confirms the new credentials actually create a session by themselves (i.e., perform or validate login). 3.ACCOVE-001 observes session-dependent UI (good), but it does not perform the login/authentication step or explicitly verify that the new password succeeded — therefore it cannot by itself confirm the credential change persisted.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: Manually: 1) Log out of the application. 2) Sign in using the NEW password set by the source test. 3) Navigate to Accounts Overview. 4) Confirm the welcome message shows the user's name and that the account list and balances are visible (account_list accessible). If successful, the password change persisted and session_status is established with the updated credentials.

coverage_gaps: The requirement (after_only) needs a test that confirms the new credentials actually create a session by themselves (i.e., perform or validate login). 3.ACCOVE-001 observes session-dependent UI (good), but it does not perform the login/authentication step or explicitly verify that the new password succeeded — therefore it cannot by itself confirm the credential change persisted.

test_cases.title: Submit Request Callback with valid inputs
test_cases.preconditions: User is signed in and the Request Callback form is displayed
steps: Fill all required fields (select Reason for Call, choose a valid Preferred Date at least the next business day, choose a Preferred Time Window, verify or edit Phone Number to a valid format)
Click "Request Callback"
test_cases.expected_result: A success message "Callback request submitted." is displayed and an email confirmation is sent.

post_verifications.ideal: Confirm a callback request record is created in Support Center with the selected reason, preferred date (at least next business day), preferred time window, and the validated phone number.
post_verifications.matched_test_title: Submit with Preferred Date set to the next business day (boundary)
post_verifications.execution_note: This candidate runs in the Support Center and its module can_verify_states include callback_request (so it targets the right data). However, as-written it performs the same create action (action_states includes callback_request_creation) rather than retrieving/observing an existing callback_request. For an after_only verification you cannot use this test as-is because it would create a new record instead of confirming the previously submitted record. To make it usable as the verification test, convert it into a retrieval/assertion test: open Support Center -> locate the callback request by confirmation ID (or view recent callback requests), then assert that reason, preferred date (>= next business day), preferred time window, and validated phone number match the submitted values.
post_verifications.reason: Not marked 'found' because the candidate performs a callback request creation (same modifies_state as the source). The after_only strategy requires a test that confirms the existing callback_request record after the action, not another submission.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: In Support Center, use the confirmation ID from the submission (or open Recent Callback Requests sorted by newest). Open the callback_request record and verify the stored fields: 1) Reason for Call matches the submitted reason; 2) Preferred Date matches the submitted date and is at least the next business day; 3) Preferred Time Window matches the submitted window; 4) Phone Number matches the validated phone number. Record the confirmation ID and store the assertions. If automating, implement a new test that queries the callback_request by confirmation ID and asserts the listed fields.

post_verifications.ideal: Verify the callback request is visible to Support Center agents by having a support_agent observer view the support queue and confirm the new callback request appears for agents to action.
post_verifications.reason: All provided candidate tests operate in unrelated modules (Manage Cards, Payments). Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate from a different module can only be considered if its can_verify_states overlaps with the source test's modifies_state (callback_request_creation). None of the candidates list callback_request or related states in their can_verify_states, so they cannot display or verify the created callback_request in the Support Center. They also do not run in the Support Center module and therefore cannot be used for cross_user observation as a support_agent.
post_verifications.suggested_manual_step: Log in as a support_agent -> Navigate to Support Center -> Open Incoming Requests / Support Queue -> Filter or search for request type 'Callback' or the submitting user's name/phone -> Locate the new callback request and confirm the displayed Reason for Call, Preferred Date/Time, and Phone match the submitted values.

coverage_gaps: Not marked 'found' because the candidate performs a callback request creation (same modifies_state as the source). The after_only strategy requires a test that confirms the existing callback_request record after the action, not another submission.

coverage_gaps: Hard module rule: the target module is Manage Cards and the required state to verify is card_control_update. All provided candidates are from different modules and their can_verify_states do not overlap with card_control_update, so they cannot observe the travel notice entries. Therefore none can be marked as a match.

All candidate tests belong to Investments or Manage Cards modules and their can_verify_states (portfolio_snapshot, fund_holdings, card_request_status, shipping_address, etc.) do not overlap with the source test's modified state (support_ticket_creation). Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate from a different module may only be 'found' if its can_verify_states overlap the modified state. They do not, so they cannot verify the Support Center attachment requirement.

The candidate runs in the correct module and touches external transfer inputs, but it only validates UI input/option behavior rather than confirming persistence of a new external transfer record. Also its module action_states overlap the source modification (external_transfer_request), so it appears to be an action/form test rather than an observation of the resulting stored record.

All three candidate tests operate on the Transfer Funds page and their module action_states include 'funds_transfer' (they exercise the transfer action rather than observe history). Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate whose action_states overlap the source test's modifies_state should not be marked 'found' because it performs the same action instead of verifying the result. None of the candidates open transfer history or a transfer-details view or search by transaction ID (they do not access/display the required transfer record or its transaction ID), so none fully satisfies the after_only verification requirement.

All candidate tests are for different modules and their can_verify_states (account_creation_status, transfer_status, payment_status, account_balance, etc.) do not overlap with the required modifies_state card_control_update. Per the HARD MODULE RULE, a candidate from another module can only be used if its can_verify_states overlap the target state; they do not. Therefore none are suitable.

Right module and touches the required state fields (card_status and card_controls), but the candidate's steps perform the Update Controls action (overlaps with the source test's modifies_state). Per the rule, a test that performs the same action cannot be marked as a full verification — it must be an observer-only test.

All provided candidates belong to other modules (Accounts Overview, Investments) and their can_verify_states do not include card_control_update. Per the Hard Module Rule, a candidate from a different module can only be considered if its can_verify_states overlaps with the source test's modifies_state (card_control_update). None do. Details: Test 3.ACCOVE-002 and 3.ACCOVE-007 are for Accounts Overview and verify account table fields (no card/travel-notice data). Test 10.INVEST-016 is for Investments and verifies plan start-date validation (no card/travel-notice data).

The candidate operates in the correct module (Investments) but does not access or display the plan detail or scheduled occurrences required by the verification. It observes portfolio snapshot data, not recurring plan schedule or next execution date, so it cannot by itself confirm the expected weekly cadence or persistence after refresh/login.

Not marked 'found' because the candidate performs a callback request creation (same modifies_state as the source). The after_only strategy requires a test that confirms the existing callback_request record after the action, not another submission.

Same module (Investments) but the test inspects portfolio snapshot rather than Order History/Trades and its action_states overlap with the source test's modified states, so it cannot, as written, confirm the existence of the executed trade record after the sell action.
